title: World Anti-Doping Agency international employees [R205(a) – C10] – Unique situations – International Mobility Program - Canada.ca
content:
## World Anti-Doping Agency international employees [R205(a) – C10] – Unique situations – International Mobility Program
This section contains policy, procedures and guidance used by IRCC staff. It is posted on the department’s website as a courtesy to stakeholders.
In these instructions, “officer” refers to employees of both Immigration, Refugees and Citizenship Canada and the Canada Border Services Agency.
The instructions on this page should be reviewed in conjunction with
- Employer-specific work permits – General processing – International Mobility Program
- Assessing genuineness of the offer of employment on work permit application – R200(5)
- Conditions and validity period on work permits
The department uses specific administrative codes to identify certain situations where it considers the work of a foreign national to create significant social, cultural or economic benefits opportunities for Canadian citizens or permanent residents, as described in paragraph 205(a) of the Immigration and Refugee Protection Regulations (IRPR). The factors provided for each code demonstrate how the situation may meet the requirements of paragraph R205(a). Officers must also always be satisfied that all requirements of section R200 are met.
The administrative code C10 is the code under which the work of certain foreign nationals entering Canada to be employed with the World Anti-Doping Agency (WADA) on a temporary basis may be described in paragraph R205(a), as it has been determined that the presence of WADA and its global headquarters in Canada results in the creation of significant social or cultural benefits. WADA’s significant presence raises Canada’s international profile, and supports Canada’s broader international commitments as part of the Olympic movement, while providing economic benefits from hosting international conventions in Canada.
- Documentary evidence
- Application assessment
- Long-term applicants and temporary purpose [R200(1)(b)]
- Final decision Approval Refusals
To be eligible under paragraph R205(a), administrative code C10, as a WADA employee, a foreign national must
- have an offer of employment from WADA for a temporary period
- be employed in an executive or manager position or in a specialized technical knowledge capacity
- have the experience and education required to meet the employment requirements of the occupation

## Documentary evidence
With the application for a work permit, officers should be satisfied that they have the following documentary evidence to make an assessment:
- an offer of employment submitted by WADA using the Employer Portal or using alternate means if authorized to do so the offer of employment must clearly describe the skills and experience required by the employee in order to perform the work sought details of the position (title, duties, duration of employment, arrangements as to payment) evidence that the applicant’s work experience and education meets the employment requirements specified in the National Occupational Classification for their occupation
- the offer of employment must clearly describe the skills and experience required by the employee in order to perform the work sought details of the position (title, duties, duration of employment, arrangements as to payment) evidence that the applicant’s work experience and education meets the employment requirements specified in the National Occupational Classification for their occupation
- the skills and experience required by the employee in order to perform the work sought
- details of the position (title, duties, duration of employment, arrangements as to payment)
- evidence that the applicant’s work experience and education meets the employment requirements specified in the National Occupational Classification for their occupation

## Application assessment
When assessing whether the work permit application meets the requirements of the significant economic and social benefit under paragraph R205(a), officers should review the offer of employment that appears under the Employment Details tab in the GCMS for information provided by the employer or the matching fields on the IMM 5802 form (if the employer is authorized to use it).
C10 This is the code that the employer selected in the Employer Portal submission of the offer. Ensure that the exemption code matches the description in “Requirements Exemptions Met.”
This is the code that the employer selected in the Employer Portal submission of the offer.

There should also be an indication that their work provides a benefit to Canada through the existence of WADA and its activities in Canada.

Applicants should provide a location of employment in Montréal, Quebec.

### Ability to perform the work sought [R200(3)(a)]
As per paragraph R200(3)(a), to issue a work permit officers must be satisfied that the foreign national is able to perform the work sought.
The officer must assess whether the foreign national meets the job requirements, including experience, education, language , training and any other elements as specified in the labour market impact assessment (LMIA)-exempt offer of employment, and whether there are any other factors that may prevent the foreign national from being able to perform the duties of the position.
For further information, officers can review the information provided in Work permit issuance prohibitions

## Long-term applicants and temporary purpose [R200(1)(b)]
Officers must be satisfied that as per paragraph R200(1)(b), the foreign national will leave Canada at the end of their period of authorized stay. Officers can review the general instructions for all temporary residents for further information.
Although applicants may be working for an international non-governmental agency or organization, they must always satisfy the officer that they will leave Canada at the end of the temporary period authorized under section R185. The applicant must be able to demonstrate that their work in Canada will be of a temporary nature.

### Occupational capacity – Executives and managers
Executives or managers employed by WADA are expected to hold positions associated with Training, Experience, Education and Requirements (TEER) categories 0 or 1.
Executive capacity (TEER 0) means that the employee primarily
- directs the management of the enterprise or a major component or function of the enterprise
- establishes the goals and policies of the enterprise, component or function
- exercises wide latitude in discretionary decision-making
- receives only general supervision or direction from higher level executives, the board of directors or stockholders of the enterprise
Managerial capacity (TEER 1) means that the employee primarily
- manages the enterprise, a department, subdivision, a component or an essential function of the enterprise
- oversees and controls the work of other managers or supervisors, professional employees or an essential function within the enterprise
- has the authority to recommend or to hire, fire or make other personnel decisions, such as promotion and leave authorization, or functions at a senior level within the hierarchy of the enterprise when they do not directly supervise employees
- exercises discretion over the day-to-day operations of the activity or function for which the employee has the authority

### Occupational capacity – Specialized technical knowledge workers
The foreign national employed by WADA as a specialized technical knowledge worker will have unique professional and technical skills that allow them to perform essential functions within the organization. They will possess abilities, knowledge and expertise that are unusual and different from those generally found in what would normally be considered the general workforce.
Employers are required to declare the NOC code and job title in the offer of employment under the International Mobility Program (IMP), as well as details of how the position and the foreign national being hired meet the requirements of the LMIA exemption for significant benefit with specialized knowledge.
Although there are no TEER category requirements, a foreign national applying in the WADA specialized technical knowledge category should be employed in a high TEER category (TEER 0, 1 or 2) to reflect that they have advanced knowledge and extensive experience. If the foreign national has a high TEER category position, an appropriate degree and less experience, a case has to be made as to how the foreign national possesses both advanced proprietary knowledge and advanced expertise.
The work permit will be issued under the authority of paragraph R205(a).
In the Global Case Management System (GCMS), on the Application screen, officers should confirm or enter the information below in the specified fields.

World Anti-Doping Agency (WADA)
Officers may issue a work permit that is valid for the duration of the offer of employment (up to 3 years for the initial work permit) or until the expiry of the travel document, whichever is earlier. If the foreign national is exempt from the travel document requirement (for example, they are a United States citizen), the work permit should be issued for the full duration of the offer of employment. Refer to Validity period for work permits .
Officers may issue a work permit that is valid for the duration of the offer of employment (up to 3 years for the initial work permit) or until the expiry of the travel document, whichever is earlier.
If the foreign national is exempt from the travel document requirement (for example, they are a United States citizen), the work permit should be issued for the full duration of the offer of employment.
Refer to Validity period for work permits .
$155 Work permit processing fee $85 Biometric fee (if applicable)
- $155 Work permit processing fee
- $85 Biometric fee (if applicable)
Biometrics are required, unless the applicant is exempt under the IRPR (for example, the 1 in 10 rule).
GCMS contains standard text for refusal grounds. Officers should ensure that the refusal grounds selected for the refusal letter match the reasons stated in their case note.
If an officer is not satisfied that all the requirements of section R200, including the assessment under paragraph R205(a) are met, they must record their reasons and outline the rationale underlying the decision as well as the facts and elements considered. They must also provide an explanation for the decision in a case note.
Refusal reasons should clearly indicate which criterion or what requirement of section R200 was not met and explain how the conclusion was reached. For assistance, officers can follow the steps in Decision making: Standard of review and process for making a reasonable decision .
The initial work permit and the extension documents can both be granted for the duration of the offer of employment (up to a maximum of 3 years per permit) or until the expiry of the travel document, whichever is earlier.
There is no limit on the number of extensions, providing the individual continues to comply with the WADA requirements, including the eligibility and admissibility considerations in paragraph R200(1)(b). For further information please review Temporary residents: Eligibility and admissibility considerations .
Significant benefit to Canada [R205(a) – C10]
